coredata.dc:title: Visible-light-driven CQDs/TiO2 photocatalytic simultaneous removal of Cr(VI) and organics: Cooperative reaction, kinetics and mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Baoxiu
Xu, Hao
Zhang, Keliu
Gao, Bo
Wang, Yilin
Wang, Qi
Zhang, Kaixin
Huang, Yue
Li, Jincheng
coredata.dc:description: CQDs/TiO2 was synthesized with a coprecipitation method and characterized by XRD, SEM, TEM/HRTEM, BET, XPS, UV–Vis DRS and I-t curve technologies. UV–Vis DRS displayed that absorption spectrum of CQDs/TiO2 enlarged to visible light zone, suggesting that CQDs/TiO2 can be irradiated by visible light. I-t curve showed that photocurrent of CQDs/TiO2 was higher than that of bare TiO2, revealing that the doping of CQDs accelerated the transfer of photoelectrons and restrained the recombination of photoinduced carriers. Simultaneous removal of Cr(VI) and organics with CQDs/TiO2 photocatalytic reaction was investigated and factors were optimized, and almost all Cr(VI) and organics were removed under the optimum conditions. Experimental results displayed that there was a distinct cooperation removal effect between Cr(VI) and organics in CQDs/TiO2 photocatalytic reaction. XPS analysis proved that Cr(VI) was reduced to Cr(III) in situ on CQDs/TiO2 surface. There were e−, h+,·OH and ·O2 − active species which were detected with DMPO in ESR test during CQDs/TiO2 photocatalytic reaction, and scavenger experiment proved that e− and h+ were the substantial reactants for Cr(VI) and organics, respectively. The pathway of photocatalytic simultaneous removal of Cr(VI) and organics underwent four steps: adsorption of Cr(VI) and organics on CQDs/TiO2 surface; production of photo electrons and holes in visible light; reduction of Cr(VI) and oxidation of organics; desorption of Cr(III) and intermediates. Photocatalytic reaction kinetics of Cr(VI) and organics were both confirmed to pseudo first-order reaction. Life span and small scale real application tests both demonstrated that CQDs/TiO2 had a potential application to wastewater containing Cr(VI) and organics.

head.ce:title: Visible-light-driven CQDs/TiO2 photocatalytic simultaneous removal of Cr(VI) and organics: Cooperative reaction, kinetics and mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Municipal Engineering, Qingdao University of Technology, Qingdao, 266033, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Municipal Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental and Municipal Engineering, Qingdao University of Technology, Qingdao, 266033, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CQDs/TiO2 was synthesized with a coprecipitation method and characterized by XRD, SEM, TEM/HRTEM, BET, XPS, UV–Vis DRS and I-t curve technologies. UV–Vis DRS displayed that absorption spectrum of CQDs/TiO2 enlarged to visible light zone, suggesting that CQDs/TiO2 can be irradiated by visible light. I-t curve showed that photocurrent of CQDs/TiO2 was higher than that of bare TiO2, revealing that the doping of CQDs accelerated the transfer of photoelectrons and restrained the recombination of photoinduced carriers. Simultaneous removal of Cr(VI) and organics with CQDs/TiO2 photocatalytic reaction was investigated and factors were optimized, and almost all Cr(VI) and organics were removed under the optimum conditions. Experimental results displayed that there was a distinct cooperation removal effect between Cr(VI) and organics in CQDs/TiO2 photocatalytic reaction. XPS analysis proved that Cr(VI) was reduced to Cr(III) in situ on CQDs/TiO2 surface. There were e−, h+,·OH and ·O2 − active species which were detected with DMPO in ESR test during CQDs/TiO2 photocatalytic reaction, and scavenger experiment proved that e− and h+ were the substantial reactants for Cr(VI) and organics, respectively. The pathway of photocatalytic simultaneous removal of Cr(VI) and organics underwent four steps: adsorption of Cr(VI) and organics on CQDs/TiO2 surface; production of photo electrons and holes in visible light; reduction of Cr(VI) and oxidation of organics; desorption of Cr(III) and intermediates. Photocatalytic reaction kinetics of Cr(VI) and organics were both confirmed to pseudo first-order reaction. Life span and small scale real application tests both demonstrated that CQDs/TiO2 had a potential application to wastewater containing Cr(VI) and organics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Let me introduce all authors and their contributions to this work. 1 Baoxiu Zhao, PhD. Prof. She devotes herself to the preparation and application of environmental friendly functional materials for water or wastewater treatment, such as photocatalyst and adsorbents. She has published many representative papers in top journal. She is the corresponding author of this manuscript. 2 Hao Xu, postgraduate. He prepared CQDs/TiO2. 3 Liuke Zhang, postgraduate. He performed structural characterizations of CQDs/TiO2. 4 Bo Gao, postgraduate. He investigated the photocatalytic reaction of CQDs/TiO2. 5 Yilin Wang, postgraduate. He established photocatalytic reaction kinetics. 6 Qi Wang, postgraduate. She analyzed photocatalytic reaction mechanism. 7 Kaixin Zhang, postgraduate. He calculated reaction rate constants. 8 Yue Huang, postgraduate. She investigated life span of CQDs/TiO2. 9 Jincheng Li, Ph. D, Prof. He performed the simulation test.

Hexavalent chromium Cr(VI) is toxic and mainly comes from chemical enterprises and treatment of chromium slag (Zhang et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2018). As a result, many tremendous environmental contaminations (Jin et al., 2016; Gheju and Balcu, 2017; Chu et al., 2019) are caused because of the wide use of chromate materials. Cr(VI) is easily absorbed by skin and respiratory systems, causing to some disasters like allergic dermatitis, eczema and pneumonia. Some investigations have stated that there are serious pathological lesions not only in skins and respiratory systems (Yu et al., 2018) but also in other systems such as blood and nervous (Kiran et al., 2016; Yoshinaga et al., 2018) when people are exposed to Cr(VI) for a long time.

In fact, heavy metal ions are not the sole pollutants in raw water resource, and other contamination caused by organic chemicals is likewise serious to environment. Lots of investigations (Sun et al., 2022) have revealed that heavy metal ions and organic chemicals almost coexist in water resource. Unfortunately, the coexistence of them may bring some severer damage to ecological environment, such as minamata disease which is caused by the coexistence of Hg2+ and methyl group erupting in Japan. Organic pollutants, such as phenol and organic acids (Dong et al., 2021) are the most common coexistence chemicals in wastewater containing Cr(VI). What should be noticed is that they also have severe toxicity, so it is necessary to remove Cr(VI) and organics simultaneously.

Up to now, adsorption or bio-sorption (Deveci and Kar, 2013; Vendruscolo et al., 2017), membrane separation (Habibi et al., 2015), electrolysis (Habibi et al., 2015) and chemical reduction are the common methods for Cr(VI). But most methods above-mentioned, such as chemical reduction and membrane separation cannot remove Cr(VI) and organics synchronously. Although adsorption is a rather method to handle with Cr(VI) and organics, it may bring potential secondary pollution. Additional, the pollution of membrane module becomes the most serious restriction in separation. So seeking a friendly and efficient method to handle with multiple contamination is of value for wastewater treatment.

Semiconductors’ photocatalytic reaction has many advantages in wastewater treatment, such as low carbon, energy saving and high efficiency (Hoffmann et al., 1995). Among these semiconductors, TiO2 is widely studied for its stability, efficiency and environmental friendship (Zhao et al., 2015; Zeshan et al., 2022). Besides owning powerful photocatalytic oxidation for organic pollutants (Zhao et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2017; Sedaghati et al., 2021), TiO2 also has efficient photocatalytic reduction for its low conduction band edge ranging from −4.0 to −4.3 eV. There are too many researches about TiO2 or other catalysts photocatalytic oxidation degradation of phenol or other organics (Niu et al., 2022) or H2 generation (Bera et al., 2021; Xue et al., 2021). Recently, some investigations (Ghosh et al., 2018; Acharya et al., 2018; Han et al., 2020; Cherifi et al., 2021) about photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) with photocatalysts have been carried out. It is reported in these references that photoinduced electrons (e−) are generated on catalyst conduction band and subsequently involved in the reduction of Cr(VI) under visible light or sunlight irradiation. However, an issue that cannot be ignored is that reduction efficiency is low because photoinduced carriers easily combine.

As we all know, the bandgap of pure TiO2 is about 3.2 eV, leading to the sole absorption in ultraviolet range, so pure TiO2 photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) only works under ultraviolet irradiation (Zeshan et al., 2022). To enlarge its absorption region to visible light zone, TiO2 is modified with different technologies. Doping nonmetallic elements such as N, F and C is a good method. Giannakas et al. (2017) synthesized N/F co-doped TiO2 and found that modified TiO2 presented higher visible light absorption than pure TiO2. In addition, construction of heterojunction is an effective way to increase visible light photocatalytic performance. Magadevan et al. (2019) prepared a novel TiO2–Cu2(OH)PO4 catalyst to reduce Cr(VI) and found that the composite catalyst exhibited high reduction efficiency in visible light irradiation, and the following UV–Vis DRS analysis further proved that the bandgap of as-prepared catalyst got narrow from 3.2 eV to 2.6 eV. Besides the above mentioned methods, dye-sensitized TiO2 also exhibited good reduction for Cr(VI) under visible light (Wu et al., 2013). Differing with those catalysts modified with doping or heterojunction, dye-sensitized TiO2 can transfer e− from dye-sensitized zone to TiO2 zone, leading to the reduction of Cr(VI) on TiO2 conduction band in visible light. However, dye-sensitized TiO2 photocatalytic reaction can cause self-decomposition of dye. So, it is necessary to add dye into system continuously, otherwise, photocatalytic reaction will suspend. Well known, e− easily vanishes or recombines h+ or other oxidative reagents, so its lifetime is very short, resulting in low photocatalytic efficiency. It seems that how to transfer e− from the conduction band of photocatalyst to other substances where e− can be stored or takes part in the reduction reaction is full of research meaning.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a kind of functional material transferring and storing electrons (Olmos-Moya et al., 2022). Khan et al. (2022) analyzed the function of CQDs in a review where extensive photonic and electronic properties, as well as light collecting, tunable photoluminescence, remarkable converted photoluminescence, and photo-induced transfer of electrons, etc. were discussed. Now, TiO2 modification with CQDs is becoming a research issue. Shafafi et al. (2020) and Zhang et al. (2021a, 2021b) prepared photocatalysts doped with CQDs and found that modified photocatalysts exhibited powerful photocatalytic performance under visible light irradiation. Xu et al. (2019) prepared CQDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite and to reduce Cr(VI) in visible light irradiation, however, the structural stability, life span and photocatalytic reduction kinetics were not researched in this work. Li et al. (2018) and Zhang et al. (2018) both investigated the role of CQDs on TiO2 photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI), where a piece of conclusion was achieved that red shift phenomenon occurred. In this work, the microstructure of CQDs/TiO2 will be through characterized to analyze the effect of CQDs dopping on TiO2 photocatalytic performance.

There are some literature about photocatalytic simultaneous removal of Cr(VI) and organics, and most of them almost focus on the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) and oxidation of organics, kinetics and mechanism. However, the researches about cooperative effect between Cr(VI) and organics, the calculation of kinetics rate constant and conservation of Cr element are not studied deeply. Furthermore, many researchers put their emphasis on simulated wastewater in lab scale, thus the investigation on application of photocatalytic simultaneous removal of Cr(VI) and organics in real wastewater is scarce. By contrast with these references, this work will focus on these issues, which is meaningful for real treatment for water or wastewater containing Cr(VI) and organics.

Titanium butoxide, glacial acetic acid, citric acid, C2H5OH, CH3COOH, K2Cr2O7, HCl, NaOH, phenol, EDTA-Na, p-benzoquinone and tert-butyl alcohol are all analytical reagents and purchased from Tianjin chemical reagent company (China). High pure water (18.2 MΩ cm−1) used in all experiments was prepared with a water purification instrument (Unique-R20, Research Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd, China). Spherical xenon lamp (150 W, Shellett Photoelectric Technology Co., Ltd, China) equipped with a filter plate is used to excite visible light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of TiO2, CQDs and CQDs/TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: TiO2 was synthesized with a sol-gel method. Detailed preparation procedure was as follows. Mixed 100 ml C2H5OH with 10 ml titanium butoxide precursor and stirred for 20 min at room temperature, forming a homogeneous mixture. Added 15 ml H2O and glacial acetic acid into the above mentioned mixture simultaneously, stirring for 40 min and obtaining the other mixture. Put this mixture into an oven where temperature was set at 100 °C, heating for 8 h and getting amorphous TiO2. And lastly, amorphous TiO2 was heated with a gradient temperature mode (0–46 min: temperature increases from 20 °C to 250 °C with a heating rate of 5 °C·min−1; 46–56 min: 250 °C was kept for an hour; 56–126 min: temperature increased from 250 °C to 600 °C with a heating rate of 5 °C·min−1; 126–186 min: 600 °C was held for an hour; 186–236 min: temperature decreases from 600 °C to 100 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C·min−1) controlled by a box-type resistance furnace.

CQDs were prepared with a hydrothermal method using citric acid and urea as the precursors, and the detailed procedure was as follows. Mixed 3 g citric acid and 6 g urea in 20 ml deionized water, stirring and getting a transparent aqueous solution, and then transferred this solution into a Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 6 h. A brown aqueous solution was obtained after reaction. This solution was centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 30 min to remove particles, and the remaining solution was then moved into a vacuum dryer to evaporate. A brownish solid was obtained finally and it was CQDs. CQDs/TiO2 was synthesized with a coprecipitation method. Mixed CQDs and TiO2 together with a certain ratio, obtaining a mixture, then put this mixture into a tube muffle furnace where temperature was set at 300 °C and argon gas was supplied to maintain an inert atmosphere, getting brown CQDs/TiO2 catalyst. CQDs/TiO2 was stored in a sealing bag before used.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was monitored using a D/max RB system (Cu Kα1 irradiation, λ = 1.5406 Å, voltage = 40 kV, current = 30 mA, scanning rate = 0.01°·s−1, scanning range = 10–80°). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were obtained using a Quanta 250FEG (FEI, accelerating voltage = 20 kV). High Resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) pictures were taken by an instrument (FEI, Talos L120C). Analysis of nonmetallic elements was obtained by an analyzer (Vario Micro, Elementar). UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectrum (UV-DRS) was obtained with an ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectrometer (LAMBDA 750, PerkinElmer) and scan wavelength ranged from 200 to 800 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was controlled by an ESCALAB250Xi instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Al Kα1 irradiation, hν = 1486.6 eV, voltage = 12.5 kV, power = 250 W, pressure = 10−6–10−7 Pa). Current-time curve (I-t) was controlled by an electrochemical workstation (ZIVE SP2). Radical species such as ·OH and ·O2 − were both tested with ESR spectrometer (Bruker EMX plus) using DMPO as the spin trapping agent.

Photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) was conducted in a 250 ml beaker on which a 150 W spherical xenon lamp equipped with a filter was placed. The aim of this filter is to remove little ultraviolet light from light resource. 200 ml aqueous solution containing 3 × 10−6 mol L−1 Cr2O7 2− and 10−6 mol L−1 phenol regarded as the representative of organics was first put into reactor, and then TiO2 or CQDs/TiO2 powder was transferred into this reactor and stirred about 30 min in dark to get an adsorption equilibrium of Cr(VI) and phenol on the surface of TiO2 and the inner of reactor. After that, light resource was turned on and photocatalytic reaction was started. Samples were withdrawn from the same place at given time internals and analyzed. Cr(VI) concentration was measured with diphenylcarbazide colorimetric method monitored by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-1900, SHIMADU). Phenol was analyzed by HPLC (LC-20AT, SHIMADU), where flow phase was composed with 70% methanol and 30% pure water, and analytical wavelength was 275 nm and flow rate was 0.8 ml min−1. Total chromium was measured by graphite furnace atomic absorption spectrometry (ICE3000, Thermo Fisher Scientific).

TiO2 was synthesized with a sol-gel method and preparation conditions were optimized with a response surface method (RSM) controlled by a Design-Expert 8.0.6 software. Compared with single-factor-experiment design, RSM can provide more interrelation information, such as the interrelation and significant order of factors. Taking account of photocatalytic reduction efficiency, four-factors-three-levels’ RSM text was designed and analysis was displayed in supply information (Tables S1–S2), where molar ratio of methanol to tetrabutyl titanate, pH value, calcination temperature and time are four main factors. It is observed from Table 2S2 that this model is very significant because p-value is 0.0029 calculated and exported by software itself. The lower the p-value is, the more significant the factor is, so a piece of conclusion can be obtained that the significant order of factors is as follows: calcination temperature > pH value > calcination time > molar ratio of methanol to tetrabutyl titanate, insisting that calcination temperature is the most important factor influencing TiO2 photocatalytic efficiency. The crystal phase of TiO2 is mainly decided by calcination temperature, and it is mainly composes by anatase when calcinations temperature is lower than 600 °C. Anatase TiO2 possesses more powerful photocatalytic performance, compared with other phases. In addition, pH value is another more important factor which affects TiO2 surface charge properties.

The interrelationship between two factors was exported by RSM software, shown in Fig. S1. It is observed from Fig. S1(a) that the interactive effect between methanol: tetrabutyl titanate and pH takes on a downward cambered surface, and the optimal values of them are 58.25 and 3.85 gotten by contour line, respectively. Similarly, the interaction caused by methanol: tetrabutyl titanate and calcination temperature (Fig. S1(b)) was also exported and optimum of them is 56.75 and 625 °C, respectively. The interactive effect between methanol: tetrabutyl titanate and calcination time (Fig. S1(c)) is presented and the optimal values of them are 57.75 and 600 °C, respectively. The constraint effect between pH and calcination temperature was displayed in Fig. S1(d) and optimum of them is 625 °C and 4, respectively. Optimum of pH and calcination time is 4 and 60 min, respectively (Fig. S1(e)), and that of calcination temperature and time are 625 °C and 60 min, respectively (Fig. S1(f)). Based on the above analysis, the optimal values of four factors were exported by software and they were as follows: methanol: tetrabutyl titanate was 67.5, pH was 4, calcination temperature and time was 600 °C and 58 min.

Loading a certain amount CQDs on TiO2 surface can widen absorption spectrum scope, resulting in red shift (Li et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018; Shafafi et al., 2020). To seek the optimal addition of CQDs, the effect of it on CQDs/TiO2 photocatalytic reduction was investigated under the optimal preparation conditions of TiO2, and result was displayed in Fig. S1(g). It is observed that photocatalytic reduction increases firstly and then decreases lastly with the enhancement of CQDs addition, and the optimal addition ratio is 4% (e.g. 1 g TiO2 powder contains 0.04 g CQDs powder), which is as same as previous researches about carbon modified TiO2 nanomaterials (Ma et al., 2019). The reason that moderate loading of CQDs can boost photocatalytic performance probably lies in two respects: one is that doping of CQDs enlarges TiO2 absorption zone from UV light to visible light, realizing visible light response, which can be interpreted by the following UV–Vis DRS spectra; and the other is that CQDs transfer e− from conduction band to surface, inhibiting the recombination of photogenerated carriers, which is proved coincidentally by the following electrochemical I-t curve. However, we found that the color of CQDs/TiO2 changes to dark from shallow white with the addition of CQDs, resulting in a serious light scattering phenomenon, and as a result, excessive loading of CQDs weakens photocatalytic efficiency.

XRD analysis of CQDs/TiO2 was performed and patterns were displayed in Fig. 1 (a). It is observed that TiO2 is mainly composed by anatase phase, for the characteristic peaks at 2θ of 25.31°, 36.97°, 37.86°, 38.63°, 48.11°, 53.93°, 55.14°, 62.23°, 62.72°, 68.75°, 70.28°, 75.13° and 76.15° correspond to (101), (103), (004), (112), (200), (105), (211), (213), (204), (116), (220), (215) and (301) planes of anatase phase, in turns, according to the standard card of anatase TiO2 (JCPDS: 89–4921). What should be noticed is that the same peaks appear in the same angles in the pattern of CQDs/TiO2, compared with those of TiO2, insisting that the doping of CQDs on TiO2 surface does not change crystal phase. According to Debye-Scherrer formula (D = Kλ/βcosθ), average size of CQDs/TiO2 can be calculated and it is about 78 nm, where K is a constant 0.59, λ is irradiation wavelength 0.15406 nm, β is half-maximum of (101) obtained via XRD software and θ is the diffraction angle.

To confirm the form of as-prepared CQDs, TEM image of it was taken and displayed in Fig. 1(b). It is observed that prepared CQDs scatter in sight and average size of them is about 5.4 nm, shown by the inset of this picture given by software. SEM image of CQDs/TiO2 displayed in Fig. 1(c) reveals that there are lots of micropores, which is good to adsorb pollutants on the surface of photocatalyst. To confirm the micromorphology of CQDs/TiO2, TEM and HRTEM were both performed and pictures were shown in Fig. 1(d and e). It is clear that there appear two crystal shapes from Fig. 1(d), demonstrating that CQDs were doped on the surface of TiO2. The following HRTEM picture shown in Fig. 1(e) exhibits that there are two different lattice spacing of 0.32 nm which is consistent with the (002) lattice surface of graphite carbon (Huang et al., 2014) and 0.19 nm which corresponds to the (100) or (010) plane lattice spacing of anatase TiO2, suggesting that CQDs are doped smoothly and CQDs/TiO2 catalyst is prepared successfully.

To obtain detailed porestructure's parameters of CQDs/TiO2, N2-adsorption-desorption experiment was carried out. Superficial area, pore volume and size were calculated with BET method and results were shown in Table S3 and Fig. 1(f). It is observed that parameters such as the superficial area, pore volume and size of as-prepared TiO2 in this work are all higher than those of P25 TiO2. Of course, a slight decline of those parameters also occurs on CQDs/TiO2. The analysis of non-metal elements reveals that the content of doped CQDs is about 1.8% in this experiment. Well known, the introduction of C elements may produce red shift (Hu et al., 2019). To verify the red shift phenomenon of CQDs/TiO2, UV–Vis DRS of it was conducted and displayed in Fig. 1(g). It is observed that absorption band of bare TiO2 mainly lies in ultraviolet scope and wavelength is 390 nm gotten via the intersection point between x-axis and tangent line of spectrum curve, revealing that bare TiO2 cannot be excited to produce e− by visible light; however, absorption wavelength of CQDs/TiO2 enlarges to 425 nm, insisting that CQDs/TiO2 can produce e− in visible light irradiation. Red shift is caused by CQDs and the reason is maybe that the C–O–Ti bonds between CQDs and TiO2 induces the lowered energy of band edge absorption and extending the absorption edge to the visible region (Hu et al., 2019). The bare TiO2 is doped with CQDs and the resultant composites performs red shift in the energy band gap decreasing from 3.2 eV to 2.9 eV. To further confirm this red shift caused by the doping of CQDs, CQDs/TiO2 photocurrent was evaluated in a three-electrode electrochemical system where TiO2 or CQDs/TiO2 deposited on the surface of Ni sheets was worked as anodes, respectively, and carbon felt and saturated calomel electrode were employed as the counter and reference electrode, and 0.1 M Na2SO4 was used as supporting electrolyte and applied potential was 0.6 V. Photocurrent density of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 irradiated by visible light was recorded by an electrochemical work station and result was presented in Fig. 1(h). It is very obvious that bare TiO2 has no photoelectric response under visible light irradiation, and moderate doping of CQDs (4%) on TiO2 accelerates the separation of photoinduced carriers. It proves that CQDs indeed act an important role in narrowing bandgap of TiO2 and transferring e−.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CQDs/TiO2 dosage and Cr(VI) concentration
ce:section.ce:para: Effect of CQDs/TiO2 dosage on photocatalytic reduction was investigated and result was displayed in Fig. 2 (a). It can be seen that photocatalytic reduction efficiency increases firstly and then decreases lastly with the enhancement of CQDs/TiO2 dosage, and the optimal dosage is 2 g L−1. When CQDs/TiO2 dosage is lower than 2 g L−1, aqueous solution containing CQDs/TiO2 power always keeps transparent, so more CQDs/TiO2 particles are excited to produce e− under visible light, leading to high reduction efficiency. However when CQDs/TiO2 dosage is higher than 2 g L−1, the turbidity of aqueous solution becomes worse and worse with the increasing of photocatalyst dosage, leading to severer light reflection phenomenon. So only partial suspended CQDs/TiO2 particles are able to receive visible light to produce e−, which results in low photocatalytic efficiency. In addition, the degradation of phenol that is usually regarded as a representative organic contamination in raw water resource was investigated during photocatalytic removal of Cr(VI). It is found that about 98% phenol is degraded by this photocatalytic reaction when CQDs/TiO2 dosage is 2 g L−1, initial concentrations of Cr(VI) and phenol are 6 × 10−6 and 1 × 10−6 mmol L−1, respectively, which proves that toxic Cr(VI) and organic pollutants can be synchronously removed by CQDs/TiO2 photocatalytic reaction in visible light irradiation.

Effect of Cr(VI) initial concentration on photocatalytic reduction was also researched and result was shown in Fig. 2(b). It is obvious that photocatalytic reduction efficiency decreases with the enhancement of Cr(VI) concentration, and it closes to 100% after 40 min when Cr(VI) concentration is 1.5 × 10−6 mol L−1. There are two reasons causing the decline of photocatalytic reduction with increasing of Cr(VI) initial concentration. One is the color of aqueous solution containing Cr(VI) which changes from light yellow to dark red with the enhancement of Cr(VI) initial concentration, inhibiting the utilization of visible light, and the other is that CQDs/TiO2 in unit concentration of Cr(VI) is less and less with the increasing of Cr(VI) initial concentration, which also decreases photocatalytic efficiency. In addition, only 86% phenol is decomposed when Cr(VI) initial concentrations is 1.5 × 10−6 mol L−1, displayed in the inset of Fig. 2(b), however, 98% phenol is degraded when Cr(VI) initial concentrations is 6 × 10−6 mol L−1 at the same time, suggesting that the coexisting of Cr(VI) and phenol boosts synergistic removal of pollutants. Detailed cooperative effect will be discussed in part 3.6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of initial pH value and atmosphere
ce:section.ce:para: pH is an important factor, because it not only affects isoelectric point of CQDs/TiO2 but also influences existing form of Cr(VI) in aqueous solution (Zhao et al., 2021). The effect of pH on photocatalytic reduction was shown in Fig. 3 (a). It is observed that photocatalytic reduction efficiency decreases with the enhancement of pH, and the optimal value is 2. In photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI), isoelectric point of CQDs/TiO2 and Cr(VI) species both change with pH value. Experiments have proved that isoelectric point of CQDs/TiO2 surface varies in different preparation procedures (Gumy et al., 2006; Arlos et al., 2016). Luo et al. (2010) prepared a nanostructured TiO2 film with electrochemical method and found that its isoelectric point was about 6.27. In our experiment, CQDs/TiO2 isoelectric point is about 6 detected with a zeta potentiometer. Hence charges on CQDs/TiO2 surface are positive when pH value is lower than 6 and negative when pH value is higher than 6. On the other hands, existing form of Cr(VI) deeply depends on pH value. Acharya et al. (2018) have investigated the relationship between Cr(VI) forms and pH value and found the following rule: H2CrO4 (pH < 2), HCrO4 − (2 < pH < 7) and Cr2O7 2− (pH > 7).

When pH value of Cr(VI) aqueous solution is lower than 2, more positive charges are concentrated on CQDs/TiO2 surface, inhibiting the adsorption of H2CrO4 molecular which is the dominant existing form of Cr(VI) in this situation, so photocatalytic reduction is lower. However, when pH value ranges from 2 to 6, H2CrO4 spontaneously ionizes to HCrO4 −, then lots of HCrO4 − anions generate, and as a result, they are easily adsorbed on the surface of CQDs/TiO2 because of coulomb attraction force, so photocatalytic reduction efficiency rises. When pH value increases from 6 to 7, CQDs/TiO2 surface becomes negative seriously, preventing HCrO4 − from being adsorbed on CQDs/TiO2 surface for coulomb repulsion force, so photocatalytic reduction declines. When pH value is higher than 7, Cr2O7 2− is the dominant form, and at the same time, CQDs/TiO2 surface becomes more negative, so adsorption becomes worse for coulomb repulsion force, resulting in lower reduction efficiency. Furthermore, it is observed that almost 99% phenol is degraded in this system when adopted pH value is 2.

Effects of N2, O2 and air on photocatalytic removal of Cr(VI) and phenol were investigated and results were shown in Fig. 3(b). It is observed that photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) works better in aqueous solution bubbled with N2 and worse in that bubbled with O2, compared with that bubbled with air. However, removal of phenol performs conversely with that of Cr(VI). In CQDs/TiO2 photocatalytic reaction, e− may be the key reagent for Cr(VI) and h+, ·OH and ·O2 − are the active actor for phenol. In O2-rich environment, e− takes part in three reactions: recombination with h+ (Eq. (1)), reduction for Cr(VI) (Eq. (2)) and self-consumption (Eq. 3-4), and the corresponding reactions are as follows:
(1) e− + h+ → hν
(2) HCrO4 − + 3e− + 7H+ = Cr3+ + 4H2O (Eφ = 1.35 V)
(3) O2 + H2O + e− = ·O2 − + OH− (Eφ = - 0.076 V)
(4) O2 + 2H+ + e− = H2O2 (Eφ = 0.695 V)
(5) H+ + ·O2 − + HCrO4 − = Cr3+ + O2 + 4OH− (Eφ = 1.27 V)

It is known that e− easily recombines with h+ spontaneously, so scavengers of h+ or e− are always added to accelerate their separation. Here, O2 is a typical trapper of e− and it can react with it to form ·O2 or H2O2 oxidative substances which can decompose organics, such as phenol in this experiment, and this is the reason that degradation of phenol performs better in O2 bubbling solution than in N2 bubbling solution. In other word, phenol acts as an effective scavenger of h+ or radicals to inhibit the recombination of h+ and e−, boosting reduction efficiency of Cr(VI). However, the fierce competition between O2 and HCrO4 − with e− weakens photocatalytic reduction efficiency of HCrO4 −. Though reductive product ·O2 − may take part in the reduction of HCrO4 − (Wu et al., 2013), as equation (5) demonstrated, the priority of e− being oxidized by HCrO4 − is higher than that by ·O2 − because of oxidation potential (1.35 V > 1.27 V). So, we can deduce that e− plays an important role in the reduction of HCrO4 −. Yuan et al. (2019) studied sunlight-driven reduction for Cr(VI) with TiO2@NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) heterostructures and also found that e− was the main active species for Cr(VI) and photocatalytic reduction efficiency increased in the presence of scavengers of h+. However, a large amount of dissolved O2 molecules are driven from Cr(VI) aqueous solution when N2 is supplied continuously, and as a result, side reactions (Eqs. (3) and (4)) consuming e− are restrained and target reaction (Eq. (2)) is boosted, so photocatalytic reduction efficiency increases.

A lot of H+ cations are involved in the reduction of Cr(VI), which has been demonstrated by the above-mentioned equation (Eq. (2)), so pH value maybe increases. To verify this hypothesis, pH value of three systems were measured and results were displayed in Fig. 3(c). It can be seen that pH values in three different reactions all increase with reaction time, and growth order is as follows: O2 > air > N2, in turns, which further proves that besides HCrO4 −, O2 molecules indeed simultaneously participate in reduction reaction of e− (Eqs. (4) and (5)). More H+ cations are consumed in O2 bubbling environment, compared with those in air or N2 bubbling aqueous solution, so pH value is highest among three reactions.

Here, a potential coprecipitation reaction between Cr3+ and OH− maybe happens during reduction reaction. If HCrO4 − is wholly reduced to Cr3+, consumed H+ cations rise sevenfold according to the equation (Eq. (2)). In this experiment, the molar concentration of HCrO4 − is nearly 6 × 10−6 mol L−1 in Cr2O7 2− aqueous solution (pH = 4), so 42 × 10−6 mol L−1 H+ is demanded. And at this moment, left H+ molar concentration is (10−4_ 42 × 10−6) mol·L−1 and then pH value is about 4.24 through calculation. At the same time, 6 × 10−6 mol L−1 Cr3+ and

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cooperative effect between Cr(VI) and organics
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the cooperative removal effect between Cr(VI) and organics, three individual experiments including Cr(VI), phenol and Cr(VI) + phenol were carried out, respectively, and results were shown in Fig. 4 (a). It is observed that the removal efficiency of Cr(VI) in single Cr(VI) and Cr(VI) + phenol aqueous solution is 80% and 99%, respectively, however, the removal efficiency of phenol in single phenol and Cr(VI) + phenol aqueous solution is 74% and 97%, respectively, proving that there is a distinct cooperative removal effect between Cr(VI) and organics. In fact, this cooperative effect caused by Cr(VI) and organics is closely related to photocatalytic redox. Organics can be photocatalytically oxidized by h+, ·OH or ·O2 − free radicals, and simultaneously, Cr(VI) can be photocatalytically reduced by e−. As a result, the coexistence of phenol and Cr(VI) inhibits the recombination of h+ and e−, boosting the removal of pollutants. Undoubtedly, e− is the sole reactant reducing Cr(VI). But in TiO2 photocatalytic reaction, there exist three kind of oxidative substances: h+, ·OH and ·O2 −. Well known, scavenger's test is a more convenient method to detect reactive radicals (Zhang et al., 2021a, 2021b; Xue et al., 2021), compared with ESR method. At present, three typical kind of scavengers of h+, ·O2 − and ·OH are EDTA-Na, p-benzoquinone and tert-butyl alcohol, respectively. To make sure which one should be responsible for the decomposition of phenol, scavenger's tests were carried out and results were displayed in Fig. 4(b). It is clear that degradation of phenol is all restrained in the presence of scavengers, however, the restriction of EDTA-Na is most serious than that of p-benzoquinone and butyl-alcohol. Hence it can be deduced that phenol is mainly decomposed by h+ in this experiment. In fact, Han et al. (2020), Liu et al. (2021) and Jin et al. (2022) investigated the simultaneous removal of Cr(VI) and organics and also proved that the substantial reactive radical for organics was h+ through scavenger's and ESR tests. To verify the presence of ·O2 − and ·OH, ESR was introduced and result was presented in Fig. 4(c). There is no any signal in dark. Obviously, two characteristic peaks of DMPO-·O2 − and DMPO-·OH appear clearly under visible light irradiation, which further affirms that ·O2 − and ·OH are generated on the surface of CQDs/TiO2 catalyst. Furthermore, the intensity of DMPO-·OH is higher slightly than that of DMPO-·O2 −, suggesting that the concentration of ·OH is higher than that of ·O2 −, which is in accord with the test of scavengers.

According to chemical reaction rate equation

, kinetics of CQDs/TiO2 photocatalytic reduction for Cr(VI) was established and effects of factors including Cr(VI) initial concentration, TiO2 dosage and pH on apparent reaction rate constant (k) were investigated. Simulation shown in Fig. 5 (a) reveals that CQDs/TiO2 photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) can be expressed well with pseudo first-order kinetics when Cr(VI) initial concentration ranges from 1.5 × 10−6 to 12 × 10−6 mol L−1 k is affected by Cr(VI) initial concentration, TiO2 dosage and pH at a certain temperature, so it can be expressed with the following function: k = k’[Cr(VI)]

To calculate the values of ɑ, β and γ, we can first fix two other factors and only change one, and then establish mathematical expression between k and variable. With above mentioned mathematical method, the effects of single factor on k were also presented in Fig. 5(b–d). The relationship between k and Cr(VI) concentration is simulated and it can be well expressed by the following mathematic function: k = 0.254 [Cr6+]−1.266 (Fig. 5(b)). So, the value of ɑ can be obtained and it is −1.266. The relationship between k and CQDs/TiO2 dosage was established and displayed in Fig. 5(c). The expression can be described by two individual functions: k = 0.0121 [CQDs/TiO2]1.4101 ([CQDs/TiO2] < 2 g L−1); k = 0.006 [CQDs/TiO2]−0.906 ([CQD/TiO2] > 2 g L−1). Namely, the value of β is 1.4101 ([CQD/sTiO2] < 2 g L−1) and −0.906 ([CQDs/TiO2] > 2 g L−1), respectively. The relationship between reaction rate constants and pH value was also displayed in Fig. 5(d). With the same mathematic simulation method, the function between reaction rate constants and pH can be expressed by the following expressions: k

Some work about the treatment of Cr(VI) single pollution or Cr(VI)/organics multiple pollution with different photocatalysts were listed in Table 1 , where important information such as Cr(VI) removal, rate constant, photocatalyst, etc. were all introduced. By contrasting with these references, we found that CQDs/TiO2 photocatalyst exhibits more prominent reduction rate than those of most reported photocatalysts in visible light.

According to characterizations and performance, photocatalytic removal of Cr(VI) and phenol with CQDs/TiO2 catalyst was deduced. HCrO4 − which is the dominant existing form of Cr(VI) in acidic aqueous solution (2 < pH < 7) spontaneously is adsorbed onto CQDs/TiO2 surface because of coulomb force, and then is reduced in situ by e− in visible light irradiation. And at the same time, organic phenol is ionized to C6H5O− and H+ in aqueous solution. C6H5O− is also easily adsorbed onto CQDs/TiO2 surface for coulomb force and is decomposed by h+. It is novel that Cr(VI) and phenol act as the scavengers of e− and h+ each other, which explains the reason that the coexistence of Cr(VI) and organic boosts the removal of them in CQDs/TiO2 photocatalytic reduction. At last, reductive product Cr3+ ions release to aqueous solution for coulomb repulsion force.

To verify the deduction, three XPS spectra including bare TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 before photocatalytic reaction (sample comes from adsorption step in dark) and CQDs/TiO2 after reaction (sample is from photocatalytic reduction step in visible light irradiation) were conducted and shown in Fig. 6 . In XPS spectrum of bare TiO2 prepared with a sol-gel method, it is observed that there are three characteristic peaks at the binding energy of 531.2 eV, 460.5 eV and 198.2 eV which are assigned to O1s, Ti2p and Cl2p elements in turns, given by software. Here, the appearance of Cl element may be caused by pollution. In XPS spectrum of CQDs/TiO2 before reaction, besides Ti and O peaks, additional two distinct peaks appear at the binding energy of 579.7 eV and 285.7 eV, and they are regarded as Cr2p and Cls. It is clear that Cr2p comes from aqueous solution containing Cr(VI) and Cls comes from CQDs doped on the surface of TiO2. However, in XPS spectrum of CQDs/TiO2 after reaction, the intensity of peak caused by Cr2p decreases sharply, suggesting that Cr(VI) is removed during photocatalytic reduction, at the same time, the peak of Cl2p also declines after reaction, insisting that Cl is also reduced synchronously.

The following fitting for Cr2p peaks coming from before and after photocatalytic reaction can explain the final fate of Cr(VI), and fitting result was displayed in Fig. 6(b). In XPS spectrum of CQDs/TiO2 after reaction, Cr2p peak marked with red diamonds is divided into two individual spectra marked with green and blue curves by fitting software. The blue line is the spectrum of Cr2p1/2 which belongs to Cr(VI) for characteristic peak appears at 579.7 eV, and the green line represents the spectrum of Cr2p3/2 which is assigned to Cr3+ for the characteristic peak appears at 576.6 eV. The inset of this figure is the Cr2p peak coming from CQDs/TiO2 before reaction, however, there is only a sole Cr2p1/2 peak which is caused by Cr(VI). So, it can be deduced that HCrO4 − is firstly adsorbed on the surface of CQDs/TiO2 and then in situ reduced to Cr3+ in visible light irradiation.

In fact, not all Cr(VI) ions are reduced and completely transformed to Cr3+, so some Cr(VI) and Cr3+ still remain on CQDs/TiO2 surface. The molar ratio of Cr(VI): Cr3+ on CQDs/TiO2 surface is about 1:4 calculated and exported by XPS software itself, revealing that Cr(VI) is reduced to Cr3+ and some Cr3+ ions are still on the surface of CQDs/TiO2 catalyst. At last, mass conservation of Cr element was roughly calculated and result was presented in Fig. 6(c). Total Cr in aqueous solution can be measured with graphite furnace atomic absorption spectroscopy and it decreases from 6 × 10−6 to 3.91 × 10−6 mol L−1, implying that other chromium ions remain on the surface of CQDs/TiO2 catalyst. In aqueous solution, Cr(VI) declines from 6 × 10−6 to 0.09 × 10−6 mol L−1 after 120 min when Cr2O7 2− initial concentration is 3 × 10−6 mol L−1. So Cr3+ ions existing in aqueous increases with reaction time and is close to 3.91 × 10−6 mol L−1 after 120 min through calculation.

Based on the above analysis of UV–Vis RDS and XPS, visible light driven CQDs/TiO2 photocatalytic removal of Cr(VI) and organics is explored. The mechanism includes major pathways: adsorption of HCrO4 − and phenol on the surface of CQDs/TiO2; production of e− and h+ on CQDs/TiO2 surface under visible light; reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr3+ by e− and oxidization of organics to intermediates by h+, ·OH and ·O2- radicals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Life span and simulation wastewater test
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the durability of CQDs/TiO2 catalyst, life span was tested and result was displayed in Fig. S2(a). In this test, used CQDs/TiO2 was filtered and dried at 100 °C for the next employment. It is observed that photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) performs well and stable in 8 running times, insisting that CQDs/TiO2 has a satisfied cycle life. This test implies that the structure and performance of CQDs/TiO2 is not changed evidently during photocatalytic reduction, which has been already proved by the comparison of XPS spectra of CQDs/TiO2 before with after reactions (see Fig. 6(a)). CQDs/TiO2 catalyst has a stable structure, so it has potential application in real wastewater treatment. To investigate the potential application of CQDs/TiO2 in real industrial wastewater containing Cr(VI) and organics, small scale simulation test was carried out in the same reactor. Main properties of wastewater such as Cr(VI) and phenol initial concentration, pH value of chemical wastewater, turbidity and conductivity, anions and cations were all listed in Table S4. It is observed from Fig. S2(b) that removal efficiencies of Cr(VI) and phenol in simulation wastewater both decrease slightly, suggesting that CQDs/TiO2 photocatalytic reaction is a satisfied method to handle with wastewater containing Cr(VI) and organics. The slight decline of removal efficiency in simulation wastewater test may be caused by co-existing ions such as Ca2+, Na+, NO3 − and Cl−.

A novel efficient CQDs/TiO2 photocatalytic reaction has been developed for wastewater containing Cr(VI) and organics and removal kinetics and mechanism were also analyzed. Almost all Cr(VI) and phenol are simultaneously removed by CQDs/TiO2 photocatalytic reaction under visible light irradiation. e− and h+ are the substantial reagents to reduce Cr(VI) and decompose organics, respectively. CQDs/TiO2 photocatalytic removal of Cr(VI) and organics both fit well with pseudo-first order kinetics. Adsorption of Cr(VI) and pollutants on CQDs/TiO2 surface, reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr3+ in situ and oxidation of organics, and desorption of Cr3+ and other intermediates to solution are involved in the removal mechanism of Cr(VI) and organics. CQDs/TiO2 owns durable and powerful ability to handle with multiple wastewater, which is of great value for the application to real wastewater.
